val_001.reasoning: The cited articles cover the legal basis for pre-trial detention (Art. 221 Abs. 1 lit. b StPO), its duration and extension (Art. 212 StPO), the requirements for extension (Art. 227, 229, 233 StPO), and the right to challenge detention (Art. 393, 396, 382 StPO). Proportionality and fundamental rights are anchored in Art. 31 Abs. 3 BV, Art. 5 EMRK, and Art. 8, 29 BV. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on pre-trial detention, proportionality, risk of collusion, and the requirements for extending detention, especially when investigative steps are largely technical or the victim has withdrawn the complaint. Recent unreported cases illustrate current application of these principles. Procedural and cost/legal aid provisions are included as standard in such decisions.

val_002.reasoning: The cited legal provisions cover the core requirements for invalidity insurance (IVG) benefits, including the definition of invalidity (Art. 7 ATSG, Art. 8 ATSG), the calculation of earning incapacity (Art. 16 ATSG), the prerequisites for rehabilitation measures (Art. 17 IVG), and the requirements for a pension (Art. 28 IVG, Art. 28a IVG). Art. 44 ATSG is cited for medical assessments, especially in cases of conflicting medical opinions. Art. 43 ATSG and Art. 19 IVG address medical measures and their exhaustion. Art. 49 and 69 IVV regulate the assessment of earning incapacity and vocational measures. Constitutional provisions (Art. 8, 9, 12 BV) are cited for equality, protection from arbitrariness, and right to assistance. Procedural articles from BGG and ATSG cover jurisdiction, standing, admissibility, and costs. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on the assessment of invalidity, the weight of medical reports (especially the distinction between specialist and treating physician opinions), the 20% earning incapacity threshold, and the principle of 'Eingliederung vor Rente' (rehabilitation before pension). Recent unreported cases address similar fact patterns involving conflicting medical opinions and the requirements for vocational measures or benefits in chronic health conditions.

val_003.reasoning: The cited articles of the StPO (Swiss Criminal Procedure Code) govern the requirements and procedure for pretrial detention (Art. 221, 212, 224-226 StPO), including the need for sufficient suspicion, risk of flight/collusion, and the process for ordering and extending detention. The cited constitutional (BV) and ECHR (EMRK) provisions guarantee the right to liberty, the right to be heard, and the presumption of innocence. The procedural articles from the BGG (Federal Supreme Court Act) and StPO regulate the admissibility, deadlines, and costs of appeals to the Federal Supreme Court. Leading BGE decisions clarify the standards for sufficient suspicion, risk assessment, the right to be heard, and the adequacy of detention reasoning. Recent unreported decisions illustrate the application of these principles to similar fact patterns. Legal aid and cost provisions are included as they are routinely addressed in such decisions.

val_004.reasoning: The cited ZGB articles cover the formal requirements for holographic wills (Art. 505 ZGB), testamentary capacity (Art. 498 ZGB), grounds for contesting a will (Art. 519, 520 ZGB), and interpretation of wills (Art. 470–472 ZGB). Art. 18 OR is included for the interpretation of declarations of intent, as is Art. 8 and 9 ZGB for the burden of proof and good faith. The cited BGG and ZPO articles cover the procedural requirements for civil appeals, deadlines, costs, and legal aid. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on the formal validity of holographic wills, testamentary capacity, the impact of language comprehension, and the standard for annulling or reinterpreting wills. Recent unreported cases (5A_...) address similar fact patterns or clarify the application of these principles. Constitutional articles (Art. 29, 30 BV) are included for due process and fair hearing, which may be invoked in inheritance disputes.

law_citations: Art. 3 Abs. 2 lit. c und d UN-Kinderrechtskonvention (KRK)
val_005.reasoning: The ZGB (Swiss Civil Code) provisions cited (Art. 133, 273, 274, 307, 308, 314 ff.) govern parental custody, visitation, the child's best interests, and the judge's ex officio fact-finding duty in child protection and visitation matters. ZPO (Swiss Civil Procedure Code) articles (Art. 296, 272, 317, 318, 106, 117) concern the non-contentious procedure, summary proceedings, evidence, and legal aid. The cited BV (Federal Constitution) articles protect equality, family life, and procedural guarantees. The UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (KRK) is directly relevant to the best interests and participation rights of the child. BGE precedents clarify the standards for restricting visitation, the evidentiary threshold for provisional versus definitive measures, the ex officio investigation duty, and the proportionality principle. Recent unreported decisions (5A_xxx/yyyy) reflect current practice in visitation restriction cases involving allegations of endangerment, sexual abuse, or substance abuse. The cited provisions and cases together address the scope of judicial investigation, the evidentiary requirements for provisional and definitive measures, and the balancing of parental and child interests.

val_006.reasoning: The case concerns whether G is liable for property damage caused during a gratuitous act of assistance, and under which legal regime (contract for work and services, mandate, or tort) and standard of care. Art. 41 Abs. 1 OR (tort) is central if no contract exists. Art. 364 Abs. 1 OR (objective standard for work contracts) and Art. 248 Abs. 1 OR (mandate, lower standard for gratuitous acts) are both relevant, as the court must decide which applies. Art. 97 Abs. 1 OR (contractual liability for non-performance) and Art. 398 Abs. 1 OR (mandate) are cited for the contractual perspective. Art. 2 ZGB (good faith), Art. 8 ZGB (burden of proof), and Art. 1 Abs. 2 ZGB (analogy) are standard in such analyses. Art. 42 Abs. 1 OR (proof of damage), Art. 97 Abs. 2 OR and Art. 99 Abs. 1 OR (causality and fault) are also relevant. Art. 100 Abs. 1 BGG, Art. 72 Abs. 1 BGG, and Art. 74 Abs. 1 lit. b BGG are procedural for civil appeals. Art. 106 ZPO covers cost allocation. BGE precedents clarify the distinction between contractual and tortious liability, the standard of care in gratuitous assistance, and the liability of non-certified actors. Recent unreported decisions are included for up-to-date jurisprudence.

val_007.reasoning:
The legal question involves (1) the validity of an inter vivos gift (Schenkung) under both New York and Swiss law, (2) the requirements for transfer of ownership of a movable (chronometer), (3) the chain of title and good faith acquisition by a third party (Alpine Trading AG), (4) evidentiary admissibility of a photocopy and foreign testimony, and (5) procedural and constitutional guarantees. 

Substantive ZGB articles cited include those on inheritance (Art. 543, 560), property and transfer (Art. 641, 714, 725, 784, 938, 939, 974), good faith acquisition (Art. 934, 936), capacity and intent (Art. 8, 16, 17 ZGB), and contract interpretation (Art. 18 OR). ZPO articles cover evidence (Art. 168, 170, 177, 179, 150, 152, 242), and BGG articles cover appeal requirements. Constitutional rights (Art. 8, 9, 29 BV) ensure equality, good faith, and due process. 

Leading BGE decisions clarify requirements for valid gifts, transfer of movables, good faith acquisition, evidentiary standards for documents and foreign testimony, and the burden of proof. Recent unreported decisions address similar fact patterns and evidentiary issues. 

The citations reflect the multi-layered nature of the dispute: inheritance law, property law, contract law, evidence law, and procedural law, as well as the interplay between Swiss and foreign law (notably, the Dead Man's Statute and the role of foreign law in Swiss proceedings).

val_008.reasoning: The core substantive provision is Art. 158 StGB (ungetreue Geschäftsbesorgung/disloyal management), including all subsections, as the facts concern a fiduciary breach by a public official in a trust context. Art. 110 Abs. 3 StGB clarifies the definition of 'Amtsträger' (public official), relevant for the public-interest aspect. Art. 12 and 13 StGB cover intent and attempt. Art. 2 Abs. 1 StGB ensures proper application of the law in time. Art. 9 BV (good faith), Art. 8 Abs. 1 BV (equality), and Art. 29/30 BV (due process, fair trial) are standard in criminal cases involving public office. Procedural articles from StPO and BGG cover appeal, remand, standing, and cost/legal aid. The cited BGE are leading cases on Art. 158 StGB, including the definition of 'Vermögensschaden', the scope of fiduciary duties, the requirements for criminal liability, and the effect of prior remand (Bindungswirkung). Recent unreported cases address similar fact patterns (conflict of interest, public trust, remand effect, indictment specificity). The indictment question invokes the 'Anklageprinzip' (requirement of precise charges), addressed in BGE 141 IV 369 and others. The remand directive and its binding effect on lower courts is treated in BGE 144 IV 1 and related jurisprudence.

val_009.reasoning: The cited ZGB articles cover the obligation to provide child maintenance (Art. 276, 277, 285), the possibility and modalities of index-linking (Art. 286), the adjustment and security of maintenance (Art. 287, 289, 290), and the possibility of capitalizing future maintenance (Art. 293, 295). ZPO articles address the procedural aspects of maintenance claims, security measures, and cost allocation. SchKG provisions (Art. 271 ff.) are relevant for freezing assets as security for maintenance claims. BGG articles cover the appeal procedure to the Federal Supreme Court. Constitutional articles (Art. 29, 8, 13 BV) may be cited regarding procedural fairness and protection of family life. The BGE and recent unreported cases cited are leading and recent decisions on the calculation of child maintenance, the impact of a debtor's lack of income (including imprisonment), the possibility of securing maintenance via asset freezing, and the capitalization of future maintenance. These citations together address both the substantive and procedural aspects of the legal question posed.

val_010.reasoning: The cited articles of the Swiss Code of Obligations (OR) cover the bank contract as a mandate (Art. 394, 398 OR), liability for breach (Art. 97 OR), exculpatory clauses (Art. 100 OR), delegation of duties (Art. 101 OR), and the effect of contractual notice/protest periods and ratification (Art. 127, 128 OR). Art. 84 OR addresses payment in foreign currency and the issue of pleading in the wrong currency. Art. 18 OR governs contract interpretation, and Art. 2 ZGB and Art. 8 ZGB concern good faith and burden of proof. Art. 29 BV and Art. 9 BV provide constitutional protection for due process and good faith, relevant to the enforceability of contractual clauses. Procedural articles from the BGG and ZPO are included for jurisdiction, admissibility, and cost allocation. The selected BGE and recent unreported decisions establish the bank’s duty of care in signature verification, the limits of exculpatory clauses, the effect of statement-hold and protest clauses, and the handling of foreign currency claims.
